key_insight: A topology optimization framework can automatically co-design hydraulic soft fish tails and their actuation pathways to produce programmable, fish-like undulating motion.
engineering_relevance: This enables more systematic design of bioinspired underwater soft robots with tunable bending, depth control, and manufacturable geometries.
method: Uses a Darcy-law-based hydraulic pressure model with a drainage term to represent spatially varying actuation loads.
Maps fluid pressure to nodal forces through finite element analysis and optimizes tail topology with robust multi-criteria objectives.
Targets deformation efficiency, fluid-structure coupling, manufacturability, and stiffness for 3D swimming kinematics.
evidence_signal: The optimized tail was computationally validated under multiple hydraulic loads, showing tunable undulatory amplitude, multiaxis bending, and better performance than a rectangular baseline.
watch_item: The approach depends on model fidelity for fluid-structure coupling and may require careful calibration before transfer to physical hardware.
paper_title: TO-SoFiT: Topology Optimization of Hydraulic Soft Fish Tail Design for programmable undulating locomotion
